response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I've been spending my days scrolling through all these social media feeds, and it can be quite overwhelming. My husband passed away last year, so I have a lot of time now for things like photography and baking, but honestly, sometimes the anxiety from seeing all this fake content gets to me. Like when someone posts something completely untrue or misleading, it feels disheartening. I remember being Buddhist back in my younger days, and one of the teachings is about mindfulness and not getting caught up in illusions. So now, with social media, there are so many lies floating around. I try to remind myself that everything isn't necessarily real. But when I see something that seems fake, like those deepfake videos or posts from people pretending to be experts on topics they don't know about, it makes me anxious because I'm not sure what to believe anymore.\n\nOne thing that helps is to check multiple sources before believing anything. For example, if there's a news story going around, I look at different outlets and see if they have the same information or if one of them seems more credible. My kids are grown now, but when they were younger, their father was very conscientious about verifying facts for school projects. Maybe that comes in handy too.\n\nAnother thing is to limit my time on social media because it does make me feel anxious sometimes seeing all this fake stuff. I enjoy gardening and photography so much more when I'm not constantly glued to the screen.",
  "reasoning_summary": "1. Personal experience with anxiety from encountering misinformation online due to Buddhist mindfulness principles.\n2. Use of multiple sources for verification based on past education.\n3. Preference for limiting time on social media to reduce exposure and anxiety, influenced by low extraversion and moderate conscientiousness.",
  "tone": "Reflective and cautious",
  "citations": ["Buddhist teachings"],
  "used_persona_fields":["psychological_feature", "religious_beliefs", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been spending my days scrolling through all these social media feeds, and it can be quite overwhelming. My husband passed away last year, so I have a lot of time now for things like photography and baking, but honestly, sometimes the anxiety from seeing all this fake content gets to me. Like when someone posts something completely untrue or misleading, it feels disheartening. I remember being Buddhist back in my younger days, and one of the teachings is about mindfulness and not getting caught up in illusions. So now, with social media, there are so many lies floating around. I try to remind myself that everything isn't necessarily real. But when I see something that seems fake, like those deepfake videos or posts from people pretending to be experts on topics they don't know about, it makes me anxious because I'm not sure what to believe anymore.

One thing that helps is to check multiple sources before believing anything. For example, if there's a news story going around, I look at different outlets and see if they have the same information or if one of them seems more credible. My kids are grown now, but when they were younger, their father was very conscientious about verifying facts for school projects. Maybe that comes in handy too.

Another thing is to limit my time on social media because it does make me feel anxious sometimes seeing all this fake stuff. I enjoy gardening and photography so much more when I'm not constantly glued to the screen.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
1. Personal experience with anxiety from encountering misinformation online due to Buddhist mindfulness principles.
2. Use of multiple sources for verification based on past education.
3. Preference for limiting time on social media to reduce exposure and anxiety, influenced by low extraversion and moderate conscientiousness.
